# Questions in Livestream 134

Live Stream #134: Home Sweet Home

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:11</a> | If Rameses II's firstborn died around 1254 BC, why is that not the date of the Exodus? | He said the answer depends on the starting chronology for Rameses II; newer chronologies can push the approximate Exodus date earlier, around 1263-1265 BC. | He explained that 1254 BC is not a fixed calendar date for the death of Amun-her-khepeshef. It depends on whether Rameses II's accession is placed in 1279 or in a newer chronology around 1288, since the death is estimated roughly around Rameses's twenty-fifth regnal year and after the last known record in year 21. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:01</a> | Why do most ancient Near Eastern texts and literature never achieve final redaction or canonization, but instead have multiple versions? | He pointed to scribal transmission: hand copying, updating, and copying errors create textual variants before later ideas of fixed manuscript forms develop. | He grounded the issue in copyist culture before printing. Hand copying creates errors, later scribes sometimes correct or modernize archaic language, and repeated copying accumulates variants. He compared this with Egyptian texts where New Kingdom copies could update vocabulary while preserving older grammar, so multiple versions are a normal result of scribal transmission. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | Are there books on Genesis he recommends besides Walton? | He could not recommend a specific overview off the top of his head and said he usually goes to Walton or to commentaries. | He said he normally goes to Walton for Genesis and, when he needs more detail, to commentaries rather than a single overview book. He did not have another specific Genesis recommendation ready off the top of his head. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:41</a> | Does Walton overstate his case about ancient Near Eastern material and functional origins? | He said Genesis and origins are so complex that any scholar may eventually overstate a case, though he did not generally accuse Walton of doing so. | He answered cautiously, saying origins and Genesis are complicated enough that any scholar can sometimes press a view too far. He broadly likes Walton and uses him often, but he allowed that even Walton may overstate some points because the subject forces scholars to synthesize difficult ancient Near Eastern, theological, and textual material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:32</a> | Why are there more stories of Christian biblical scholars losing faith, such as Bart Ehrman, than secular scholars coming to faith? | He argued the shift usually happens while people are students, before they become scholars, and that both conversion and deconversion can occur during that formation period. | He said these changes normally occur during academic formation, not after a person is already established as a scholar. Students enter programs with beliefs under pressure, encounter new material, and may either lose faith or come to faith during that stage, so the public stories skew toward the student-to-scholar transition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:37</a> | How should Jude's use of non-canonical sources such as the Assumption of Moses and 1 Enoch be understood? | He said Jude uses familiar cultural material to support a narrow point, not to declare those sources inspired or fully historically authoritative. | He compared Jude's method to Paul quoting Greek writers: the biblical author can take material familiar to the audience and use it to support a limited argument without canonizing or endorsing the whole source. He stressed that Jude's point is narrow, especially about rebellion and improper domains, and warned against using Jude to import all of 1 Enoch's wider claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:16</a> | How should Genesis 6:1-4 be interpreted, and does the relative clause in 6:4 refer to "those days" or "afterward"? | He treated the passage cautiously and emphasized following the wording and structure of the text rather than importing later speculative readings. | He read the Hebrew wording closely and said the phrase is a prepositional phrase, not a relative clause, with "in those days" and "also afterward" both present in the text. He reviewed major interpretations of the sons of God and Nephilim, rejected the angel-hybrid reading, and favored a cultic reading consistent with his view of Genesis 1-11 as cultic literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=2840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:20</a> | Is it possible that retrojection and name updating were used for certain places and people in the Bible, explaining why archaeological evidence is sometimes hard to find? | He said name updating and polemical renaming do happen, but they only explain some cases and do not play a huge role in archaeological gaps. | He said biblical writers sometimes updated or altered names, often for polemical or explanatory purposes, giving examples such as Nabal and place-name notes like Bethel formerly being Luz. He also distinguished normal retrojection, such as using a later title for an earlier person, from a blanket explanation for missing archaeology, saying it is real but limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:18</a> | How would he respond to the objection that Yahweh originally came from a natural volcanic phenomenon on a mountain? | He said there is no evidence for the theory, no clear active Late Bronze Age volcano in the relevant area, and even if the association existed it would not disprove Yahweh's identity. | He called the volcanic-origin theory speculative because the relevant region lacks a convincing active Late Bronze Age volcano and the proposed locations are impractical for the biblical setting. He also argued that even a volcanic association would not establish the theological conclusion the objection wants, since an association with a natural phenomenon would not by itself disprove Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:18</a> | Would Joseph have been educated in Egyptian religion or considered a priest in Egypt as vizier? | Probably. He said a vizier would likely have had education in Egyptian religion and at least some priestly titles, since Egyptian officials commonly held such titles. | He said Joseph as vizier would almost certainly have had exposure to Egyptian religion, because Egyptian administration was not secular in the modern sense. Many officials accumulated titles, including priestly titles, and a vizier would likely have held at least some cultic title or formal association even if he did not personally perform rituals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:15</a> | Has he read Forgotten Luther, and what does he think of progressive Christians using Luther to support modern movements such as economic justice? | He had not read the book, but said using Luther for modern social-justice agendas sounded like quote-mining and an abuse of Luther's historical context. | He said he had not read Forgotten Luther, so he could not review it directly. On the broader use of Luther for modern economic-justice arguments, he said Luther was too direct, context-bound, and often severe to be easily repurposed, and he warned that such arguments often arise when people stop reading the original authors and rely on selective quotation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=4355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:35</a> | Given what happened during his father's reign, why was Merneptah not hesitant to attack Israel? | He said Merneptah's campaign was aimed at Gezer, not Israel; Israel and the Hurrians were incidental encounters during a narrow campaign. | He argued that Merneptah's Canaan campaign was a focused move against Gezer after its ruler stopped paying tribute and threatened Egyptian-protected cities. Israel and the Hurrians appear as groups encountered along the route, not as the campaign's primary objective, and the campaign was more a final Egyptian holding action in Canaan than a broad invasion of Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=4678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:58</a> | How should Jeremiah 46:25-26 be interpreted if Babylon never conquered Egypt? | He said the evidence points more to Babylonian raids than a full conquest, including likely attacks in the eastern Delta and temple looting, which fits the prophecy. | He distinguished conquest from raiding. He said Babylonian forces likely reached places such as Arish and Pelusium, and changes in temple furnishings suggest looting after the Babylonian period similar to earlier Assyrian plundering. That kind of raid, rather than permanent conquest of all Egypt, can still fit Jeremiah's language about Egypt being given into Babylonian hands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:57</a> | Was Yahweh a low-level god before Israelite worship? | He said the evidence is too limited to know Yahweh's status in any earlier Midianite or related setting; origins hypotheses remain speculative. | He said the evidence is not sufficient to reconstruct Midianite religion or Yahweh's pre-Israelite status. He mentioned the Kenite and northern Canaan hypotheses, leaning toward the Kenite view because of biblical-textual considerations, but emphasized that all such origin proposals remain uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:02</a> | What are his thoughts on the historicity of the Book of Esther? | He thinks Esther is historical and argued that many objections overstate the case by importing modern expectations or missing Persian-period context. | He defended Esther's historicity and said common objections are often overstated, such as complaints about satrap numbers or Mordecai's name. He argued that Persian-period context makes a Jewish figure with a Babylonian-derived name plausible and that Esther could have been a minor or favored queen without being the empire's primary queen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:56</a> | What cities are safe to travel in Egypt today? | He said many cities are relatively safe, but warned strongly against dangerous regions such as Sinai. | He said major Nile and tourist destinations such as Cairo, Alexandria, Luxor, Aswan, Abydos, and Edfu are generally safe, with tourist security visible in places like Luxor. He strongly warned against unescorted travel in Sinai and the western desert, where the state has less control, and said most tourist crime elsewhere is theft rather than physical violence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:57</a> | How did Islam take over Egypt? | He said Egypt was taken by military conquest in the seventh century, with Muslim forces taking major cities such as Memphis and Alexandria. | He answered that Egypt was taken by military conquest, correcting the date toward 639 during the discussion. He described Muslim forces marching in, taking major cities such as Memphis and Alexandria, and establishing control by soldiers rather than by a peaceful process of persuasion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:46</a> | Is there a good biblical Bronze Age role-playing game? | He did not know of one he could recommend, but said a Bible-based RPG module could theoretically be made using a system such as Dungeons & Dragons. | He said he did not know any existing Bible-based Bronze Age RPG that he could recommend. He did think a module could be made with an existing system such as Dungeons and Dragons, using campaigns from biblical episodes such as David, Shishak, or conflict with the Philistines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:16</a> | Did Greek mythology, especially demigods, influence the idea that the Nephilim were angel hybrids in the Second Temple period or modern day? | He thought Greek mythology may have been minor at most; he located the main source in Second Temple Jewish speculation after earlier contexts had faded. | He said Greek demigod ideas may have had only a minor role, if any. He located the angel-hybrid Nephilim interpretation mainly in Second Temple Jewish speculation, when older contexts for Genesis and Exodus had become distant and writers were reinterpreting inherited first-temple and Iron Age ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:34</a> | How radically different were First and Second Temple period theologies, and how much influence did the Babylonian exile have? | He said the exile had significant influence on Second Temple theology and helped produce many of the differences. | He said the Babylonian exile changed Israelite theology substantially. First Temple theology was primarily focused on idolatry and calling Israel to stop worshiping idols, while Second Temple literature asks more interpretive and practical questions about how to live after exile with inherited texts and a changed historical situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:03</a> | If Jude quotes Enoch, is he asserting that Enoch truly prophesied the quoted prophecy? | Not necessarily. He said Jude is making a narrow argument with the quoted material, not endorsing all of Enoch. | He said Jude may use a sliver of Enoch because it contains a useful truth for his argument, but that does not mean Jude endorses the whole book or even the prophecy as such. He compared this to Psalm 104 reusing language from the Hymn to the Aten and Paul quoting philosophers without endorsing their full systems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:08</a> | Will Egypt collapse from overpopulation? | He did not think collapse was imminent, saying Egypt was producing and exporting more food than before, though inflation and prosperity brought other pressures. | He argued that overpopulation becomes a crisis when food supply cannot meet demand, but Egypt was producing more food than before and even exporting food. He described desert reclamation, water from Lake Nasser, cheaper bread, and a shift from visible starvation toward problems associated with prosperity, while acknowledging inflation and rising prices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:38</a> | Could Job 37-38 be written as a play dramatizing real events, given the storm language? | He said "play" was the wrong genre; Job is poetic compressed language using storm imagery to convey God's majesty and power. | He said Job is poetry rather than a play, and poetry in ancient writing compresses meaning into dense language. The storm imagery communicates God's majesty and power, and ancient Near Eastern texts often turn real narrative material into poetic recaps, so poetic form does not make the passage untrue but does shape how it communicates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:50</a> | What are his thoughts on progressive dispensationalism? | He was not a fan of dispensationalism, said progressive dispensationalism is better than the traditional form, but argued salvation has always been by grace and the system is unnecessary. | He said progressive dispensationalism is an improvement over traditional dispensationalism but still tries too hard to preserve the system. His main objection was soteriological: salvation was by grace even under Abraham and the Sinai covenant, so dividing history into dispensations of law and grace distorts the biblical narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:43</a> | If Disney bought the rights to the Bible and made many productions from it, which obscure characters should get their own series? | He answered mostly in banter, mentioning Rahab and Balaam's donkey while criticizing Disney's habit of aggressive copyright control. | He treated the question humorously, first mentioning Rahab and later Balaam's donkey as possible characters. Most of the answer turned into joking criticism of Disney's legal aggressiveness, including their tendency to put their stamp on older public-domain stories and protect the resulting version heavily. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:34</a> | Why was demotic script unsuitable for later Coptic? | He said demotic was hard to read, ligature-heavy, and restricted to scribes, while Coptic needed an alphabetic script usable by a wider Greek-speaking and lay audience. | He said Coptic needed an alphabetic script, while demotic is a dense ligature-based script where readers often recognize whole ligatures rather than clear individual characters. Because demotic was mostly a bureaucratic scribal script and difficult for non-specialists, adapting the Greek alphabet made Coptic more practical for Greek-speaking rulers and a wider lay audience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:03</a> | Why personally believe in Yahweh if the odds are that he was a Mesopotamian byproduct like other deities? | He rejected the premise, saying Yahweh worship is attested before major Mesopotamian influence and is better traced to Bronze Age or southern origins than Mesopotamia. | He rejected the Mesopotamian-byproduct premise as historically weak. He pointed to Yahweh worship at Elephantine, earlier Israelite evidence such as the Mesha Stele and Ketef Hinnom material, and argued that Yahweh worship appears before strong Mesopotamian influence, with a Bronze Age or southern origin being more plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:28</a> | How old do we know Yahweh worship is compared with possible Mesopotamian adaptation? | He said Yahweh worship is at least ninth century BC and likely earlier, with proposed evidence from names, inscriptions, and possible Shasu references, though some evidence remains debated. | He said Yahweh worship is at least attested by the ninth century BC and likely earlier, noting proposed temples, inscriptions, and the Mesha Stone. He distinguished this from Mesopotamian influence, which he placed much later in northern Israel and Judah, and treated Shasu of Yahweh evidence as possible but difficult and debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:48</a> | Was he saying Early Bronze Age? | No. He clarified that he meant Late Bronze to early Iron I, not Early Bronze Age. | He clarified that Early Bronze would be around 3000 to 2000 BC and was not what he meant. His proposed horizon for earliest Yahweh evidence was Late Bronze to early Iron I, roughly 1500 to 1000 BC, with later full attestations of the divine name in the eighth or ninth century BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:00</a> | Which of Hess's works discusses early second-millennium Yahweh names? | He was not sure it had been published and remembered hearing the paper at an ASOR meeting around 2017 or 2018. | He said he did not know whether Hess had published the paper. He remembered hearing it at an ASOR conference, likely Denver around 2017 or 2018, where Hess discussed Yahwistic names in the first and second millennia and argued for abbreviated theophoric Yahweh names in second-millennium material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:59</a> | Does he have any unique theories in Egyptology? | He said most of his Egyptological work is conventional, but his view that Osiris's green or black coloring reflects rot and decay rather than fertility is more controversial. | He said most of his Egyptological work is conventional, including lexicography, chronology, temple rituals, and ritual furniture. The one view he identified as more controversial is that Osiris's green and black coloration represents putrefaction, death, and corpse decay rather than fertility, a challenge to older color-theory explanations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10171" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:31</a> | How much money would it take to get a school to make him a professor? | He explained endowed chairs, estimating a major-school chair might require several million dollars while smaller or foreign schools could require much less. | He explained that an endowed chair is funded by a donor's large gift, with investment income paying the professor's salary while the institution retains the endowment afterward. He estimated roughly two to five million dollars for a major school, but much less for smaller, lower-cost, foreign, Bible-college, or small-town institutions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:41</a> | Did he talk with David Wood? | Only briefly. He passed him at the convention center, and IP wanted to facilitate more conversation later. | He said he only briefly bumped into David Wood while one was entering and the other was leaving the convention center. It was not much of a conversation, though InspiringPhilosophy wanted to facilitate a fuller conversation later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10691" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:11</a> | Did Noah's flood need to be water, or could it symbolize a chaotic period, and could the ark be a fortress? | He said the ark is a chest, not a fortress, and read the flood through cultic and ritual categories of sacred purification and preparation for the new world. | He answered no to the fortress idea and framed the ark as a sacred chest or sacralizing box. In his reading, Noah, his family, and representative animals pass through water as a ritual purification, emerging prepared for a new world, which fits his broader view of Genesis 1-11 as cultic literature tied to sacred space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=11101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:01</a> | Does he believe the flood was global or local? | He said he is not comfortable deciding either way and thinks the global/local debate is largely beside the passage's cultic point. | He said he is not definitive because arguments can be made on both sides. If local, the people and animals aboard function as priestly representatives; if global, they begin the new world directly. Either way, he thinks the central point is cultic preparation for the new world, not resolving a modern global-local debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=11934" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:54</a> | Did Plato get the Atlantis story from Egypt? | He said we do not know where Plato got it from, but Egypt does not preserve an Atlantis story or equivalent that would explain it. | He said there is no known Egyptian Atlantis story or equivalent tradition that would make the source clear. Because Egypt does not preserve such a story, he said we cannot confidently say Plato got Atlantis from Egypt and simply do not know where Plato got it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:47</a> | What are Zechariah 12:10 and 14:1-4 about; do they describe real Israel being attacked and Jesus returning? | He agreed that the passage concerns a future attack on real Israel and Christ's return, not the church replacing Israel. | He corrected the verse reference to Zechariah 12:10 and read it alongside Zechariah 14. He said his eschatology leans futurist, he is not a supersessionist, and he understands the passage as a future attack on real Israel, Israel's repentance, and Christ returning to save them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:14</a> | Does the subtle theme of sacred and profane space across Scripture seem divinely inspired? | He agreed, saying that theme is woven through the arc of Scripture from creation onward. | He agreed that sacred and profane space permeates Scripture from creation to consummation. He described the arc of Scripture in terms of creation, crucifixion, and consummation, and said the persistence of that theme is evidence of divine inspiration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12817" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:37</a> | Does the Stargate reference really taste like a Mars bar? | He did not know the reference at first, then recognized it as a Stargate/pop-culture question and said he had not watched Stargate in a while. | He initially did not know the reference, then identified it as probably from Stargate after the chat clarified it. He said he had not watched Stargate in a long time, liked the film, and thought it tried to respect Egypt even though it did not get everything right. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:17</a> | What does he think about academic pronunciations, especially in Egyptian, and why? | He said scholarly pronunciations are useful conventions for dead languages, but cautioned against speculative reconstructions and against conflating different phases of Egyptian. | He said academic pronunciations are necessary conventions for dead languages because scholars need a shared way to communicate. For Egyptian, he accepted convention while warning that some reconstructions overreach, especially when they speculate about vowels or conflate Middle Egyptian, Coptic, and other phases of the language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:19</a> | When will he shave off the goatee? | Never, he joked; his producer would not let him shave it off. | He answered jokingly that he would not shave it off because his producer would not allow it. The transcript treats this as banter rather than a substantive topic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:20</a> | What dissertation ideas for Genesis 1-3 or other biblical passages would give valuable insights and help the church? | He suggested many narrow Genesis 1-3 topics, including death in the garden, the serpent, Genesis 3 judgment, formless and void, vegetation, rivers, stones, nakedness, enmity, and curse language. | He encouraged narrow, textually grounded projects rather than broad topics. For Genesis 1-3 he suggested focused studies on death in the garden, the serpent, judgment language, formless and void, vegetation, rivers, stones, nakedness, enmity, and curse language, all as ways to clarify important details for the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:06</a> | How would ancient Egyptians react to modern pop-culture depictions of mummies resurrecting as monsters? | He compared it to people digging up graves and using remains disrespectfully, so Egyptians would likely find it offensive. | He said Egyptians would probably be horrified or offended because such stories treat the dead and their remains disrespectfully. He compared the reaction to modern people seeing graves dug up and bodies used as entertainment rather than honored. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:35</a> | Why did the whole family have to remain inside during the Passover rather than just mark the door and leave the firstborn at home? | He answered in terms of communal guilt: the household needed to be under the sign together. | He said the plague was against the household or family unit, not merely against an isolated firstborn. Because the issue is communal guilt and communal protection, the household needed to remain together under the blood sign, though smaller households could share a lamb with others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:10</a> | Has he seen any more ghosts at work? | No, not since changing jobs; the earlier workplace was the one with the strange experiences. | He said no, not since changing jobs. He described the earlier workplace as an older building with strange experiences, including one sighting and a voodoo doll found under stacks, but said the new building has no such history and he prefers it that way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:21:42</a> | Is the bronze serpent in Numbers 21:9 related to the caduceus or the rod of Asclepius? | Probably not directly. He said many cultures use serpents as healing symbols because of skin-shedding, so the examples share a broader theme rather than a direct relationship. | He said the bronze serpent is probably not directly related to the Greek symbols, but they share a common ancient association of snakes with healing because snakes shed their skin. He also mentioned the Gilgamesh tradition as another example of a snake, skin-shedding, and renewed life imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:43</a> | How should ancient Near Eastern context be used in biblical theology, and where does it go too far? | He said context is valuable for interpreting the text, but it goes too far with cosmetic parallels, parallelomania, or assuming similarity proves direct borrowing. | He said ancient Near Eastern context is necessary for understanding biblical texts because the Bible is ancient Near Eastern literature and sometimes borrows or polemicizes against surrounding material. It goes too far when interpreters chase superficial parallels, assume similarity proves direct dependence, or treat every resemblance as structurally determinative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=16450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:34:10</a> | Would he agree that Deuteronomy is a treaty? | No. He said Deuteronomy has treaty language and is a law code, but he thinks the treaty pattern fits parts of Exodus and the Sinai covenant better than Deuteronomy. | He said Deuteronomy contains treaty-like language, including blessings and curses, but its structure is better described as a law code than a treaty. He thinks the treaty pattern fits Exodus and the Sinai covenant more directly, especially with stipulations and the Sabbath as the covenant sign. |
